system.name: Citizens United Education Platform
system.mission: Educate—not merely inform. Education is primary; leadership is the outcome.
system.core_question: How did one Supreme Court decision change American politics?

ladder.title: Discover
ladder.description: Learn why Citizens United still matters.

ladder.title: Understand
ladder.description: Grasp what happened and what changed.

ladder.title: Explore
ladder.description: Drill into history, law, data, and sources.

front_door.title: What happened?
front_door.L1: In January 2010, the Supreme Court decided Citizens United v. FEC. The Court ruled that the government cannot restrict independent political spending by corporations and unions — treating much of that spending as protected free speech under the First Amendment.

front_door.title: Why it matters
front_door.L1: The decision removed key limits on how wealthy individuals, corporations, and outside groups can influence elections. It helped fuel Super PACs, massive independent expenditures, and ongoing fights over disclosure and corruption.

front_door.title: How money changed after 2010
front_door.L1: Outside spending in federal elections rose sharply. Super PACs — which can raise unlimited sums — became major players. Much spending flows through nonprofits with limited disclosure, creating the 'dark money' debate.

front_door.title: Who gained power
front_door.L1: Wealthy donors, corporate treasuries, unions, and well-funded outside groups gained more ability to shape elections independently of candidates. Critics say this amplifies elite voices; supporters say it expands who can participate in political debate.

front_door.title: What people are doing now
front_door.L1: Reform efforts include constitutional amendments, federal legislation, state ballot initiatives (like Montana), state corporate-law approaches (like Hawaii), disclosure pushes, and litigation testing what states can still regulate.

front_door.title: How you can help educate your community
front_door.L1: You do not need a law degree to help neighbors understand this case. Host a conversation, teach a short class, research your state, or become a local education lead. The goal is people who can explain Citizens United to others.

halls.title: The Story Before Citizens United
halls.purpose: Show that this did not come from nowhere.

halls.title: The Case Itself
drilldowns: Why did the FEC get involved?
What question did the Court answer?

halls.title: The Debate
halls.purpose: Build credibility by showing both sides clearly.
